perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees not to use the other's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the specific consultation regarding the subject matter of this Agreement. Any other use of such Confidential Information shall be made only upon the prior written consent from an authorized representative of the party which disclosed such information (hereinafter the "Disclosing Party") or pursuant to subsequent agreement between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may, at its discretion, use the other's Confidential Information for purposes reasonably related to the consultation regarding the subject matter of this Agreement. Any other use of such Confidential Information may be made with the Disclosing Party's consent, which should not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "agrees not to use" with "may, at its discretion, use... reasonably related" introduces ambiguity. The phrase 'reasonably related' is subjective and open to interpretation. The phrase 'should not be unreasonably withheld' places an ambiguous burden on the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets. The original text strictly prohibits unauthorized use. The modified text allows use based on a subjective interpretation of 'reasonably related' and weakens the consent requirement, making it more difficult to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, which violates the intent of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intent of trade secret protection. The original text provided a strict prohibition against using confidential information for purposes outside the agreement. The changed text, by using 'may, at its discretion, use... reasonably related' introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection. The phrase 'reasonably related' is subjective, allowing for potential unauthorized use of confidential information. This contradicts the purpose of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which aims to protect against misappropriation, as indicated by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act under Civil Code sections 3426, et seq., implying a legal framework exists to protect trade secrets, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that from the date of receipt, the party receiving the Confidential Information (hereinafter the "Receiving Party") shall not disclose Confidential Information to any other person, firm, corporation or other entity or use it for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall not publish, divulge, communicate, or reveal any Confidential Information to any person, corporation, or other third party or to any of Receiving Party's employees who do not have a need to know such Confidential Information with respect to their job duties.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that the Receiving Party should endeavor not to disclose Confidential Information to any other person, firm, corporation or other entity or use it for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement. The Receiving Party will attempt to limit the publication, communication, or revelation of any Confidential Information to third parties or to its employees who may not require such Confidential Information for their job duties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall not disclose" to "should endeavor not to disclose" and "shall not publish, divulge, communicate, or reveal" to "will attempt to limit the publication, communication, or revelation" weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The word 'endeavor' suggests an effort rather than a strict requirement. 'Attempt to limit' is also weaker than a strict prohibition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The weakened language creates ambiguity and makes it harder to prove misappropriation under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original text provided a clear standard of non-disclosure, while the modified text introduces subjectivity and makes it more difficult to enforce confidentiality.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall not disclose" to "should endeavor not to disclose" and from "shall not publish, divulge, communicate, or reveal" to "will attempt to limit the publication, communication, or revelation" weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. This introduces ambiguity into the contract, making it more difficult to enforce confidentiality, and therefore creates a legal contradiction under the definition provided for 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation'. The provided snippet from the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426) establishes the legal basis for trade secret protection, and the modification weakens the protection that the original contract offered.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information and copies and extracts thereof shall be promptly returned to Disclosing Party three years from the effective date of this Agreement, or at any time within thirty (30) days of receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party for the return of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information and copies thereof should generally be returned to the Disclosing Party approximately three years from the effective date of this Agreement, or within a reasonable time after receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be promptly returned' to 'should generally be returned' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. 'Approximately three years' and 'reasonable time' are vague terms that allow for discretion and are subject to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The ambiguity surrounding the timing of the return of confidential information undermines the protection afforded by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original text provided a specific timeframe which ensured the information was safeguarded in a timely manner. The modified version introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be promptly returned' to 'should generally be returned' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. Furthermore, the change from a specific timeframe ('three years') to vague terms like 'approximately three years' and 'reasonable time' makes it difficult to enforce compliance. Although the provided law snippet only cites the title of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), the Law Explanation suggests that the ambiguity undermines the protection afforded by the act, creating a contradiction by potentially hindering the timely safeguarding of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted under principles of law common to commercial agreements, with reference to the spirit of California law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing from explicit 'governed by the laws of the State of California' to 'generally interpreted under principles of law common to commercial agreements, with reference to the spirit of California law' introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what 'principles of law common to commercial agreements' are, and 'spirit of California law' is vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1636 states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. The modified text moves away from a concrete and known body of law (California law) to vague 'principles of law'. This conflicts with the clear directive to follow California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly states that it 'will be governed by the laws of the State of California.' This is a clear and unambiguous designation of the applicable law. The changed text replaces this with a vaguer standard of 'principles of law common to commercial agreements, with reference to the spirit of California law.' This introduces ambiguity and moves away from the explicit directive to follow California law, as explained in the Law Explanation. This directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1636, as detailed in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, which mandates that a contract 'must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful.' By shifting the governing law to a set of 'principles' and the 'spirit' of California law, the modified text makes the ascertainment of the parties' intention more difficult and undermines the binding force of California law itself, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: If any action or proceeding is brought for the enforcement of this Agreement, or because of an alleged or actual dispute, breach, default, or misrepresentation in connection with any of the provisions of this Agreement, the successful or prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and other costs incurred in such action or proceeding in addition to any other relief to which it may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: If any action or proceeding is brought for the enforcement of this Agreement, or because of an alleged or actual dispute, breach, default, or misrepresentation in connection with any of the provisions of this Agreement, the court may, at its discretion, award reasonable attorneys' fees and other costs to a party deemed to have acted in good faith.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the successful or prevailing party shall be entitled to recover' with 'the court may, at its discretion, award' and adding 'to a party deemed to have acted in good faith' introduces ambiguity and makes the recovery of attorney's fees uncertain. The original text provides a clear entitlement, while the revised text makes it discretionary and dependent on a subjective assessment of 'good faith'.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 1033.5 speaks to costs in general, contract clauses that *mandate* attorneys' fees to the prevailing party are generally enforceable in California. By changing 'shall be entitled' to 'court may, at its discretion' and adding 'good faith', the modified language makes the recovery of attorney's fees uncertain, potentially discouraging parties from enforcing the agreement and contradicting the implied intent of the original provision, which was to make the prevailing party whole. It introduces uncertainty where the original language provided relative certainty, creating a potential conflict with how California courts interpret and enforce contracts that mandate attorney's fees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1033.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates attorney's fees to the prevailing party, while the changed language makes it discretionary based on 'good faith'. This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, potentially discouraging enforcement of the agreement. CCP 1033.5(a) outlines allowable costs, and while it doesn't explicitly mandate attorney's fees, the change from 'shall be entitled' to 'court may, at its discretion' undermines the original intent to make the prevailing party whole, thus creating a legal contradiction.
